plainMeaning: The Jina's body shines with rows of auspicious signs—parasol, fly-whisk, banner, barley-sheaf, makara, horse, śrīvatsa, svastika, bull, lion, chariot and wheel—even mountains, oceans and continents among them.
recitation: chatta cāmara paḍāga jūva java maṁḍiā, jhayavara magara turaya sirivaccha sulaṁchaṇā. dīva samudda maṁdara disāgaya sohiā. satthia vasaha sīha-raha-cakka varaṁkiā.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit chatta < Sanskrit chatra; one of the eight maṅgalas and a mark of sovereignty.
wordByWord.notes: First of the maṅgala-lakṣaṇa series listed in this verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit cāmara, from camarī (the yak); an emblem of royal honour borne beside the Jina.
Prakrit paḍāga < Sanskrit patākā; sign of victory.
Prakrit jūva < Sanskrit yūpa, the ritual stake; here an auspicious figure among the foot-marks.
wordByWord.notes: Reading uncertain; some take jūva as a metrical pairing with the following java.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit java < Sanskrit yava 'barley', an ancient symbol of plenty.
Prakrit maṁḍiā < Sanskrit maṇḍita, past participle of √maṇḍ 'to adorn'; closes the first group of marks.
wordByWord.notes: Predicate adjective qualifying the Jina (understood as the implied subject).

wordByWord.etymology: jhaya (< Sanskrit dhvaja, 'flag') + vara ('excellent, foremost'); flag distinct from the earlier paḍāga.
Sanskrit makara, the mythic crocodile-fish; a standard maṅgala emblem.
Prakrit turaya < Sanskrit turaga 'swift-goer, horse'.

wordByWord.gloss: śrīvatsa (the auspicious curl-mark of the chest)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sirivaccha < Sanskrit śrīvatsa; the emblem of glory borne on a great being's breast.
su- ('good, fair') + laṁchaṇa (< Sanskrit lāñchana, 'mark, emblem'); sums up the second group as 'well-marked'.
Prakrit dīva < Sanskrit dvīpa; the ring-continents of Jain cosmography.
Prakrit samudda < Sanskrit samudra; the seas alternating with the dvīpas.
Sanskrit mandara, name of the central cosmic mountain.
disā (< Sanskrit diś, 'direction') + gaya (< Sanskrit gaja, 'elephant') = diggaja, the elephants warding the quarters.
Prakrit sohiā < Sanskrit śobhita, past participle of √śubh 'to shine'; closes the cosmological group.
Prakrit satthia < Sanskrit svastika, from svasti 'well-being'; foremost of the aṣṭamaṅgala.
Prakrit vasaha < Sanskrit vṛṣabha 'bull'; emblem of steadfastness (and of Ṛṣabha, the first Jina).
sīha (< siṁha, 'lion') + raha (< ratha, 'chariot') + cakka (< cakra, 'wheel'); three emblems compounded by the metre.
wordByWord.notes: Printed as a single conjunct cluster in the book; resolved here into its three members.

wordByWord.etymology: vara ('excellent') + aṁkiā (< Sanskrit aṅkita, 'marked, stamped', from √aṅk); final predicate gathering the whole catalogue.

literalTranslation: Adorned with parasol, fly-whisk, banner, sacrificial-post and barley; bearing fair emblems—the excellent banner, makara, horse and śrīvatsa; made resplendent with continent, ocean, Mount Mandara and the directional elephants; stamped with the excellent signs of svastika, bull, lion, chariot and wheel.
idiomaticTranslation: His very body is a treasury of auspicious signs—graced with parasol, fly-whisk, banner, sacrificial post and barley-sheaf; fair-emblemed with the proud flag, the makara, the horse and the śrīvatsa; resplendent with continents, oceans, Mount Mandara and the elephants of the quarters; and stamped with the noble marks of svastika and bull, lion, chariot and wheel.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This stanza is a pure catalogue—an epithet-shower of the auspicious signs (maṅgala-lakṣaṇa) read upon the Jina's body and, in the tradition, upon the soles of his feet. The poet organises some twenty emblems into four metrical waves, each sealed by a participle of beauty: maṁḍiā ('adorned'), the implicit sulaṁchaṇā ('fair-marked'), sohiā ('resplendent'), and finally varaṁkiā ('stamped with excellent signs'). The sweep deliberately mixes registers: court-regalia (parasol, fly-whisk, banner, flag), ritual and rural plenty (yūpa, barley), heraldic creatures (makara, horse, bull, lion), and—most strikingly—whole cosmological structures (continents, oceans, Mandara/Meru, the diggaja-elephants of the quarters). To find islands and the world-mountain numbered among bodily marks is the verse's quiet boldness: the Jina's person is figured as a microcosm, his frame legible as the very map of the universe. Coming after the long tableau of the worshipping celestial women (vv.26–31), the hymn now turns from those who adore the Jina to the adorable body itself, beginning the marks-and-purity sequence the next stanzas complete. The diction is compressed to the point of pure enumeration—no finite verb, no connective—so that the marks accumulate like a litany, each name a fresh point of darśana for the reciter to rest the eye and mind upon.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The lakṣaṇas catalogued here are the bodily signs of a mahāpuruṣa—the supremely meritorious frame that fruits from oceans of accumulated puṇya and marks a being destined for Jinahood. Several items (svastika, śrīvatsa, makara, banner, parasol, fly-whisk) overlap the aṣṭa-maṅgala, the eight auspicious symbols of Jain worship, so the verse implicitly identifies the Jina's body itself as the locus of all auspiciousness. The cosmological marks (dvīpa, samudra, mandara, diggaja) carry a subtler doctrinal charge: the omniscient Jina, whose kevala-jñāna encompasses the entire loka, is fittingly imaged as bearing that loka upon his person—knowledge and its object meeting in one form.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For the reciter this verse is a guided darśana: as each emblem is named, the mind paints it upon the Jina's body, turning chanting into visualised worship (bhāva-pūjā). Dwelling on a frame composed wholly of auspicious signs is itself held to be maṅgala—steadying the attention and inviting the protective, fear-dissolving aim of this smaraṇa.

crossReferences.text: Aupapātika-sūtra — description of the Tīrthaṅkara's body bearing the auspicious lakṣaṇas
Triṣaṣṭi-śalākā-puruṣa-caritra — the auspicious bodily marks of a śalākā-puruṣa
Aṣṭa-maṅgala overlap (svastika, śrīvatsa, makara, etc.) in Jain iconography

sources.title: Shrivatsa: 39 definitions (Jainism, Sanskrit, Prakrit)
sources.consultedFor: Meaning and Jain iconographic sense of śrīvatsa (sirivaccha).

sources.title: Diggaja, Dish-gaja: 16 definitions
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of disāgaya/diggaja as the directional world-guarding elephants.

sources.title: Jain stotra anthologies / Ajitaśānti Stava recensions
sources.consultedFor: Identifying this stanza as the auspicious-marks (lakṣaṇa) catalogue within the Ajitaśānti Stava.

notes: Token jūva is read as yūpa ('sacrificial post'); some recensions/readings treat the jūva–java pair differently, so this gloss is moderately uncertain. The printed conjunct સીહરહચક્ક is resolved as the three emblems siṁha (lion) + ratha (chariot) + cakra (wheel). The verse has no finite verb; the Jina is the understood subject carried over from the preceding stanzas. Orthography (anusvāra, doubled conjuncts, the spaced ॥ daṇḍas and terminal period) follows the book verbatim and is not normalized.
